content: Antiepileptic drug (AED) selection depends on seizure type, patient factors, and side effect profile. Focal seizures: first-line includes lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine. Generalized tonic-clonic: valproate (most effective but teratogenic), lamotrigine, levetiracetam. Absence seizures: ethosuximide (first-line for pure absence), valproate, lamotrigine. Myoclonic seizures: valproate, levetiracetam, topiramate. Key considerations: (1) Drug interactions—carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital are CYP450 inducers reducing efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, many medications. (2) Teratogenicity—valproate highest risk (neural tube defects, cognitive impairment); avoid in women of childbearing potential. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have lower teratogenic risk. (3) Titration—lamotrigine requires slow titration to prevent Stevens-Johnson syndrome. (4) Monitoring—phenytoin has nonlinear kinetics; carbamazepine causes hyponatremia; valproate requires LFT/platelet monitoring. Refractory epilepsy (failure of 2 appropriate AEDs) warrants epilepsy center referral for surgical evaluation, vagal nerve stimulation, or responsive neurostimulation.
